name: Asmarathya
dates_notes: Pre-Bādarāyaṇa; cited in Brahma-Sūtra 1.2.29 and 1.4.20; no work survives.
dating_methodology: No biographical text survives. Terminus ante quem: Āśmarathya is named in the *Brahma-Sūtra* (1.2.29; 1.4.20; cf. 4.4.6 by reference) as one of three earlier authorities Bādarāyaṇa juxtaposes; he must therefore antedate the *Brahma-Sūtra*'s redaction, conventionally placed circa 200–100 BCE (Nakamura, *A History of Early Vedānta Philosophy*, 1949, vol. I; Deussen, *The System of the Vedānta*, 1883, with later refinement). Terminus post quem: he must be late enough to engage the developed *jīva-Brahman* problematic of the principal Upaniṣads. The window 400–100 BCE is therefore a defensible range, not a precise estimate. The standard placement of Āśmarathya as the eldest of the *adhikaraṇa*-cluster (Āśmarathya — Auḍulomi — Kāśakṛtsna at 1.4.20–22) follows from the order of citation, which Nakamura argues is doxographically structured rather than chronological, but which Suthren-Hirst (*Śaṃkara's Advaita Vedānta*, 2005) treats as preserving genuine sequencing. Note (AI-generated audit): every dating claim about Āśmarathya is inferred from his place in the citation-cluster; no independent attestation exists. The dating-window followed here is the consensus of Nakamura and Sharma (*The Brahma-Sūtra*, 1965) for the citation-only proto-Vedāntins as a cohort.
core_thesis:
Āśmarathya holds that the *jīva* is non-different from Brahman *as its real manifestation*, and different from Brahman *as the manifested mode is not the unmanifest source* (*Brahma-Sūtra* 1.4.20 *abhivyakter ity āśmarathyaḥ*; cf. 1.2.29). The position is *bhedābheda* before that name existed; what is distinctive in Āśmarathya, on the citation-evidence, is the appeal to *abhivyakti* (manifestation) as the structural reason that both relations hold simultaneously and non-trivially. At *Brahma-Sūtra* 1.2.29 — *abhivyakter ity āśmarathyaḥ* — the position is glossed as resolving a particular interpretive problem: how can the same Brahman be both within the heart (the *daharavidyā* of *Chāndogya-Upaniṣad* 8.1.1–6 and similar Upaniṣadic passages) and beyond all spatial limit (cf. *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad* 3.8.8)? Āśmarathya's answer, on Śaṅkara's reading (*Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* on 1.2.29), is that the manifestation-relation makes the apparently inconsistent predicates consistent: Brahman as manifest within the *jīva*-locus is genuinely there, and Brahman as unmanifest source is not exhausted by that manifestation.

The claim has a precise structural form. Āśmarathya is committed to four propositions taken together (so the doxographic reconstruction in Nakamura, *A History of Early Vedānta Philosophy*, 1949, vol. I, ch. 4): (i) the *jīva* is genuinely non-different from Brahman, since it is the manifestation of Brahman (*Brahma-Sūtra* 1.4.20); (ii) the *jīva* is genuinely different from Brahman, since the manifested mode is conditioned and the source is not (entailment from the same sūtra read against 1.4.21); (iii) the relation of manifestation is itself real, not a metaphor for either an underlying identity or an underlying duality (the force of *abhivyakti* as a substantive structural relation; so Bhāskara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* on 1.4.20); (iv) the *upādhi*-mediation that produces the apparent distinctness is part of the constitution of the *jīva* qua *jīva*, not an extrinsic accident (a contrast Bhāskara presses at *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* on 2.1.14 against the Auḍulomi-line). The fourth point is what distinguishes Āśmarathya from Auḍulomi. Auḍulomi takes the embodied modes to drop at *mokṣa*, so that real distinctness obtains only in the bound state and identity stands forth at liberation (*Brahma-Sūtra* 1.4.21 *utkrama-iṣyate-evaṃ-bhāvāc-ity auḍulomiḥ*; 4.4.6). Āśmarathya does not concede the same temporal asymmetry; the manifestation-relation, on his view, is a structural feature of *brahman* as such, not a feature of the bound *jīva* alone (so the contrast preserved at *Brahma-Sūtra* 1.4.20–21). This is what later *bhedābheda* will systematize as the simultaneous-and-non-cancellable holding of both relations.

The position is also dialectically distinct from the developed *bhedābheda* of Bhāskara (ninth century, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* on 2.1.14) and Nimbārka (twelfth century, *Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* on 2.3.42). For Bhāskara, the *jīva* is genuinely *bhinna* in the bound state and *abhinna* at liberation, with the difference belonging to *upādhi* and dropping at *mokṣa* (Bhāskara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* on 2.1.14, the *upādhi*-thesis) — closer to Auḍulomi than to Āśmarathya. For Nimbārka, both *bheda* and *abheda* are equally fundamental, ontologically irreducible, holding for the *jīva* always and not merely at one phase of its career (Nimbārka, *Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* on *Brahma-Sūtra* 2.3.42; *Daśa-Ślokī* v. 4 *svabhāvataḥ*). Āśmarathya, on the citation-evidence, is closer to Nimbārka in structural commitment than to Bhāskara: the manifestation-relation does not collapse at *mokṣa* into bare identity (so Bhāskara's own gloss on *Brahma-Sūtra* 1.4.20, where Āśmarathya is read as the *bhedābheda*-precedent). He is also distinct from the later Caitanya-school *acintya-bhedābheda* (Jīva Gosvāmī, *Sarva-Saṃvādinī* and *Tattva-Sandarbha* §§44–47), which appeals to the inconceivability of the relation; Āśmarathya gives the relation a positive name (*abhivyakti*) and treats it as conceivable — manifestation being a structure familiar from Vedic ritual and Upaniṣadic cosmology (cf. *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad* 1.4.7 on *vyākaraṇa* of name-and-form).

What the citation does not preserve is Āśmarathya's account of *how* the manifestation produces real difference without compromising the source. Bādarāyaṇa's adjudication at *Brahma-Sūtra* 1.4.22 sides (Śaṅkara argues, in *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* on 1.4.22) with Kāśakṛtsna's strict identity; on Bhāskara's reading (*Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* on 1.4.22), the same silence is endorsement of Āśmarathya's position. The historical Āśmarathya cannot be made to settle the dispute (Nakamura 1949, vol. I, ch. 4). He committed to *bhedābheda*-by-manifestation; the further metaphysical specification is filled in by the schools that later claim him.

engaged_works.title: Positions reconstructed from Brahma-Sūtra citations
engaged_works.ascription_notes: No work survives; the bhedābheda-leaning position is reconstructed from Brahma-Sūtra 1.2.29 and 1.4.20.
engaged_works.summary: Three sūtra-loci preserve Āśmarathya, and the philosophical content of each is grammatically tight. *Brahma-Sūtra* 1.2.29 — *abhivyakter ity āśmarathyaḥ* — invokes him on the question of how Brahman can be predicated as 'within the heart' (*Chāndogya-Upaniṣad* 8.1.1, *daharavidyā*) and 'beyond all measure' (*Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad* 3.8.8) simultaneously. The single word *abhivyakter* (genitive of *abhivyakti*, manifestation) carries the doctrine: the within-the-heart predication and the beyond-all-measure predication are both literal, because manifestation is a real ontological relation in which the manifest is the source without exhausting it (so Śaṅkara's gloss at *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 1.2.29). *Brahma-Sūtra* 1.4.20 — *abhivyakter ity āśmarathyaḥ* (the locus where Āśmarathya appears as the first voice in the three-position cluster on the *jīva-Brahman* relation, with Auḍulomi at 1.4.21 and Kāśakṛtsna at 1.4.22) — applies the same manifestation-thesis to the *jīva*: the *jīva* is genuinely Brahman as manifest, and genuinely conditioned as the manifestation is not the unconditioned source. *Brahma-Sūtra* 4.4.6 (*saṅkalpāḍ-eva-tu-tac-chrutyāt*, in the cluster discussing the *mukta*'s capacities) places Āśmarathya implicitly within the doxography of liberation, where his position contrasts with Auḍulomi's *mokṣa*-state non-difference (*Brahma-Sūtra* 1.4.21): for Āśmarathya the manifestation-structure does not collapse at liberation. Śaṅkara reads Āśmarathya as offering a provisional doctrine subordinate to the absolute non-difference reading (*Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 1.4.20–22); Bhāskara (*Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* on 1.4.20) and Yādava-Prakāśa (as reconstructed from Rāmānuja's polemic at *Vedārtha-Saṅgraha* §22) read him as their pre-Bādarāyaṇa authority for *bhedābheda*; Rāmānuja's school treats him as compatible with qualified non-dualism (*Śrī-Bhāṣya* on 1.4.20). The historical position firmly attestable across these readings is the manifestation-grounded simultaneous *bheda-and-abheda*; the further doctrinal specification is post-citation (Nakamura 1949, vol. I).
